text:
RESTAGING CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST (PANCREATIC PROTOCOL)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old female with known borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate process adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12V mutant, ATM germline pathogenic variant. Initially staged as T4 N1 M0 with SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees and SMV contact with partial luminal narrowing. CA 19-9 at diagnosis 1,247 U/mL, declining to 620 U/mL after two cycles; now 310 U/mL after 4 cycles. Undergoing neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX. Presented for interval response assessment.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed on a 64-slice multidetector scanner following administration of 120 mL of iohexol (Omnipaque 350) intravenous contrast. Acquisitions obtained in the late arterial (pancreatic parenchymal) phase and portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal reformations generated. Thin-section axial series reconstructed at 1.25 mm intervals through the pancreatic bed with dedicated review of perivascular anatomy. No oral contrast administered per pancreatic protocol. Comparison made to prior multiphasic CT obtained at the time of initial diagnosis.

FINDINGS:

Pancreas:
There has been meaningful interval reduction in the size of the hypoenhancing mass centered in the pancreatic head and uncinate process. The lesion now measures approximately 3.1 x 2.5 cm in the axial plane (previously 3.8 x 3.2 cm on the diagnostic study), representing a perceptible decrease across all measured dimensions. The mass remains hypoenhancing relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on both arterial and portal venous phase acquisitions, with a less prominent desmoplastic halo than appreciated on the prior examination. The margins of the tumor appear somewhat less infiltrative in character, though the lesion retains the irregular, spiculated morphology characteristic of ductal adenocarcinoma.

The main pancreatic duct proximal to the mass demonstrates residual mild dilation, measuring approximately 4 mm in greatest caliber (reduced from 6 mm on the prior study), consistent with partial relief of ductal obstruction. The pancreatic parenchyma upstream of the tumor shows mild volume loss with some fatty replacement changes, likely reflecting progressive atrophy in the setting of chronic ductal obstruction.

Vascular Relationships:
Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): On careful multiplanar review, tumor contact with the right lateral wall of the SMA has decreased substantially. The arc of soft tissue abutting the vessel circumference is now estimated at approximately 90 to 120 degrees — a notable improvement from the greater-than-180-degree circumferential contact documented at baseline. The fat plane between the tumor and the SMA, while not fully restored, is partially apparent on several consecutive axial and coronal images. No definitive arterial encasement or luminal irregularity of the SMA is identified. The proximal SMA remains patent with preserved luminal caliber.

Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV) and Portal Confluence: The SMV retains residual soft tissue contact at its right anterior surface at the level of the uncinate process; however, the previously noted mild luminal narrowing has improved. The venous lumen appears adequately preserved in caliber on portal venous phase images with no teardrop deformity or collateral venous formation to suggest hemodynamically significant obstruction. The portal vein confluence is patent. The splenic vein appears normal in caliber.

Celiac Axis and Hepatic Artery: No involvement of the celiac axis or proper hepatic artery. Normal origins and caliber. No arterial encasement.

Lymph Nodes:
The previously noted peripancreatic lymph node along the superior mesenteric vessel pedicle has decreased in short-axis diameter to approximately 0.7 cm (previously 1.2 cm), no longer meeting size criteria for suspicion of nodal involvement. No new enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the peripancreatic, celiac, retroperitoneal, or pelvic regions.

Bile Ducts and Gallbladder:
The common bile duct measures approximately 9 mm in caliber (previously 12 mm), with modest improvement in biliary decompression. Mild residual intrahepatic biliary prominence is noted, predominantly involving the left lobe; this is stable to slightly improved. The gallbladder appears unremarkable without wall thickening, stones, or pericholecystic fluid. No biliary stent is in place.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on either the arterial or portal venous phase. No hepatic cysts, hemangiomas, or hypervascular or hypovascular lesions to suggest metastatic involvement. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in appearance without nodularity or enlargement.

Kidneys:
Bilateral kidneys enhance normally and symmetrically. No hydronephrosis, cortical lesions, or perinephric abnormality. Mild cortical thinning bilaterally, likely representing age-related change.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity, omental thickening, or mesenteric soft tissue implants to suggest carcinomatosis. The mesenteric fat planes appear clean without stellate retraction or seeding.

Bowel:
Small and large bowel loops are within normal limits for caliber and wall thickness. No obstruction or pneumatosis. The duodenum appears patent.

Bony Structures:
No destructive osseous lesions identified within the visualized skeletal structures. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine consistent with age.

IMPRESSION:

1. Interval reduction in the size of the pancreatic head/uncinate process adenocarcinoma, now measuring approximately 3.1 x 2.5 cm compared with 3.8 x 3.2 cm at initial staging — a meaningful decrease in tumor bulk following four cycles of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX, consistent with a partial radiographic response.

2. Improved vascular geometry at the superior mesenteric artery: the arc of tumor-vessel contact has decreased to an estimated 90-120 degrees of circumference, compared with greater-than-180-degree contact at baseline. While a clear fat plane has not been fully restored, the anatomic relationship is substantially more favorable than at presentation.

3. Improved SMV patency: residual soft tissue contact at the SMV is present but the previously appreciated luminal narrowing has resolved. No collateral formation.

4. The previously suspicious peripancreatic lymph node has decreased to 0.7 cm and is no longer convincingly enlarged.

5. Modest improvement in common bile duct caliber and mild reduction in upstream pancreatic ductal dilation, reflecting partial decompression of the biliary and ductal systems.

6. No hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or ascites. No new sites of disease.

Overall, these findings reflect a favorable oncologic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The evolving vascular anatomy warrants continued multidisciplinary assessment regarding timing and feasibility of surgical resection.

Signed electronically by: Attending Abdominal Radiologist, Section of Body Imaging
